status_notes: Initial research from Google Cloud Secret Manager and Certificate Manager documentation, 2026-04-04. Pass 1 (service docs). Needs Pass 2 (API), Pass 3 (cross-reference), and Pass 5 (human review with live GCP environment).
official_name: Google Cloud Secret Manager and Certificate Manager
notes: Secrets management and TLS certificate provisioning for GCP.

match_nodes.description: Store secrets with automatic versioning and encryption

data.description: Store sensitive data such as API keys, passwords, certificates, and other secrets. Secrets are encrypted at rest using Google-managed encryption keys by default, or customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) for additional control. Each secret can have multiple versions. Supports replication policies: automatic (Google-managed) or user-managed (specific regions). Maximum secret size: 64 KiB per version.

options.description: Default encryption. Google manages the encryption keys.
You provide Cloud KMS keys for encryption. Full key lifecycle control.
Google manages replication across regions.
You specify which regions to replicate the secret to.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use Secret Manager instead of hardcoded credentials or environment variables. Use CMEK for secrets requiring key rotation control. Configure replication policies based on data residency requirements.

match_nodes.description: Immutable secret versions with lifecycle management

data.description: Each secret value is stored as an immutable version. Versions can be in one of three states: Enabled (accessible), Disabled (not accessible but can be re-enabled), or Destroyed (permanently deleted after a configurable delay). Applications can access a specific version or the latest version. Version numbering is sequential (1, 2, 3, ...).

options.description: Active and accessible. Default state for new versions.
Not accessible but data preserved. Can be re-enabled.
Permanently and irreversibly deleted. Cannot be recovered.

data.vendor_recommendation: Disable old versions before destroying to allow a grace period. Use the latest alias for applications that should always use the newest version. Pin specific versions for applications that need stability.

data.description: Fine-grained IAM access control on individual secrets. Key roles: Secret Manager Secret Accessor (read secret values), Secret Manager Secret Version Manager (manage versions), Secret Manager Admin (full management). IAM policies can be set at the project level (all secrets) or per individual secret for granular control.

options.description: Access secret versions (read secret values). Most commonly granted to applications.
Manage secret versions (add, enable, disable, destroy). Does not include read access.
Full management of secrets and versions.
View secret metadata (not values). List secrets.

data.vendor_recommendation: Grant secretAccessor only to service accounts that need to read secret values. Use per-secret IAM policies for sensitive secrets. Avoid granting admin role broadly — use viewer and secretVersionManager for management tasks.

match_nodes.description: Secret rotation via Pub/Sub and Cloud Functions

data.description: Secret rotation using Pub/Sub notifications and Cloud Functions. Configure a rotation schedule on the secret; when the schedule triggers, a message is published to a Pub/Sub topic. A Cloud Function subscribed to the topic performs the actual rotation (create new credential, update secret version, update target service). No built-in rotation — you implement the rotation logic.

options.description: Configurable rotation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days). Triggers Pub/Sub notification.
Receives rotation notification messages.
Implements rotation logic: generate new credential, add secret version, update target system.

default.note: Rotation must be explicitly configured with a schedule, topic, and function.

data.vendor_recommendation: Implement rotation for all database credentials and API keys. Test rotation logic thoroughly in non-production environments. Use the rotation schedule feature rather than external schedulers for auditability.

match_nodes.description: Managed TLS certificates for Load Balancers

data.description: Managed TLS certificate provisioning and lifecycle management for Google Cloud Load Balancers. Supports Google-managed certificates (automatically provisioned and renewed) and self-managed certificates (bring your own). Certificate maps define which certificates to serve for which hostnames. DNS authorization for domain validation.

options.description: Automatically provisioned and renewed by Google. Supports DNS authorization (wildcard) and load balancer authorization (single domain).
Upload your own certificate and private key. You manage renewal.
Map certificates to hostnames. A single load balancer can serve multiple certificates.
Prove domain ownership via DNS CNAME record. Supports wildcard certificates.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use Google-managed certificates with DNS authorization for automatic provisioning and renewal. Use certificate maps for multi-domain configurations. Use self-managed certificates only when specific CA requirements exist.

data.description: Google Cloud Certificate Authority Service (CAS) for creating and managing private certificate authorities. Issue private X.509 certificates for internal services, devices, and workloads. Supports CA hierarchies (root and subordinate CAs), certificate templates, and IAM-based access control. Two tiers: DevOps (basic, lower cost) and Enterprise (higher assurance, Cloud HSM backed).

options.description: Software-backed keys. Lower cost. Suitable for internal service certificates.
Cloud HSM-backed keys. Higher assurance. Suitable for regulated environments.
Groups of CAs for load balancing and availability. Certificates are issued from a pool.
Define allowed key usages, extensions, and identity constraints. Enforce certificate policy.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use CA pools for high availability. Use certificate templates to enforce certificate policies. Use Enterprise tier for production environments requiring HSM-backed keys. Implement a CA hierarchy with offline root CA.

audit.console: Cloud Console → Security → Certificate Authority Service
